source.organizationName: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

content: Blood pressure is the force of blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. It's measured with two numbers: systolic (top number) and diastolic (bottom number). Normal blood pressure is less than 120/80 mmHg. High blood pressure (hypertension) is 130/80 mmHg or higher. Understanding your readings: Systolic pressure measures the force when your heart beats. Diastolic pressure measures the force when your heart rests between beats. Why it matters: High blood pressure makes your heart work harder and can damage blood vessels, increasing risk of heart attack, stroke, and kidney disease. How to measure at home: 1) Sit quietly for 5 minutes before measuring. 2) Sit with back supported, feet flat on floor, arm at heart level. 3) Take 2-3 readings one minute apart and record the average. 4) Measure at the same time each day. When to call your doctor: If your reading is 180/120 or higher, wait 5 minutes and recheck. If still high, seek immediate medical care. If you have symptoms like chest pain, shortness of breath, or vision changes, call 911.

citationFormat.citationText: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute. (2023). Understanding Your Blood Pressure. Retrieved from https://www.nhlbi.nih.gov/health/high-blood-pressure
